nodes.description: Dr Rashidianfar noted that Toplace conceded the property was uninhabitable during this period, affecting his ability to rent it out.

nodes.title: [2023-05-22 13:28] Toplace appealed on questions of law under NCAT Act
nodes.description: Toplace stated that it is appealing on questions of law as per Section 80(2)(b) of the Civil and Administrative Tribunal Act 2013 (NCAT Act).

nodes.title: [2023-05-22 13:28] Toplace did not identify precise nature of errors in Tribunal's decision
nodes.description: Toplace has not identified with particularity the precise nature of the errors it submits the Tribunal Member made in his decision regarding damages.

nodes.title: [2023-05-22 13:28] Toplace argued Tribunal applied wrong principle of law to loss of rent

nodes.title: [2023-05-22 13:28] Tribunal made an error of law regarding assessment of lost rent

nodes.description: Toplace argued that damages cannot be recovered for any loss which could have been prevented by reasonable mitigation action of the injured party.

nodes.description: The Member’s reasons did not specifically refer to mitigation, although it was noted that Toplace argued Dr Rashidianfar should have moved his goods from the property to prevent damage.

nodes.title: [2023-05-26 06:08] Brewarrina Shire Council v Beckhaus Civil Pty Ltd & 1 Or [2005] NSWCA 248 is a case concerning a dispute over a contract for the construction of flood levees
nodes.description: Case summary: Brewarrina Shire Council v Beckhaus Civil Pty Ltd & 1 Or [2005] NSWCA 248 involves a dispute between a local council and a civil construction company regarding defective work, delay, and termination of the contract for flood levees and associated works.

nodes.title: [2023-05-26 06:08] Main issues in Brewarrina Shire Council v Beckhaus Civil Pty Ltd & 1 Or [2005] NSWCA 248 include validity of contract termination and contractor's right to claim for work done
nodes.description: Main issues: 1. Validity of termination of the contract by the council and contractor's right to claim for work done prior to termination. 2. Application of the Building and Construction Industry Security of Payment Act 1999 (NSW) in relation to progress payments and right to suspend work. 3. Availability of liquidated damages for delay and defective work under the contract and council's right to claim for such damages. 4. Appropriate costs orders in the case.

nodes.title: [2023-05-26 06:08] User understands significance of termination of contract in homeowner's NCAT claim
nodes.description: Significance of termination of contract: A homeowner may face difficulties in claiming damages for defective or incomplete work if the building contract has not ended. Only delayed completion damages can be awarded while the building contract remains on foot. The contract must end, either by valid termination or acceptance of repudiatory conduct. Termination allows the homeowner to claim damages for defective or incomplete work.

nodes.title: [2023-05-26 06:08] User notes that repudiatory conduct occurs when one party breaches a fundamental term of the contract
nodes.description: Repudiatory conduct: Occurs when one party breaches a fundamental term of the contract or indicates an intention not to perform their obligations. Examples include refusing to complete necessary work, failing to meet deadlines, refusing to rectify defective work, and abandoning the project.

nodes.title: [2023-05-26 06:08] User recognizes that termination of a building contract can be carried out by mutual agreement or unilateral action
nodes.description: Termination: The legal process by which either party ends the contract can be mutual or unilateral. Proper process must be followed, including giving written notice and complying with notice periods required by the contract.

nodes.title: [2023-05-26 06:08] User understands that once a building contract is terminated, they may claim damages for defective or incomplete work
nodes.description: Effect of termination: Once a building contract has been terminated, homeowners may claim damages for any defective or incomplete work carried out prior to termination, as termination ends the builder's right to carry out further work.

nodes.title: [2023-05-26 06:08] Blessed Sydney Constructions PL v Vasudevan case considered the impact of Brewarrina principle on renewal power
nodes.description: Case background: Blessed Sydney Constructions PL v Vasudevan involved a building dispute where the owner sought to terminate the contract and claim damages for breach. The Appeal Panel held that the orders available on a renewal application were not constrained by the Brewarrina principle.

nodes.title: [2023-05-26 06:08] Types of orders available on renewal application in Blessed Sydney Constructions PL v Vasudevan include rectification and money orders
nodes.description: Types of orders: The Appeal Panel held that a money order was available on renewal under HBA Sch 4 para 8, including rectification orders, money orders, and any other order that the Tribunal could have made when the matter was originally determined.

nodes.title: [2023-05-26 06:08] User acknowledges the importance of electing to terminate in Blessed Sydney Constructions PL v Vasudevan
nodes.description: Electing to terminate: The owner elected to terminate the contract and sought a money order on a renewal application, allowing the Tribunal to make orders unconstrained by the Brewarrina principle. Homeowners need to understand the implications of electing to terminate and seeking damages.
